title: The Role of Forgiveness in Spiritual Awakening
meta_description: Forgiveness is not a requirement for spiritual growth. It is a destination you reach when ready, not a box to check. Forcing forgiveness before processing pain blocks authentic awakening. Your anger and grief are valid. Honor them first.
og_title: The Role of Forgiveness in Spiritual Awakening
og_description: Forgiveness is not a requirement for spiritual growth. It is a destination you reach when ready, not a box to check. Forcing forgiveness before processing pain blocks authentic awakening. Your anger and grief are valid. Honor them first.
body:
You Don't Have to Forgive What Happened to You

Let me say that again because it might hit you sideways: you don't have to forgive what happened to you. Not yet. Maybe not ever.

I know that sounds like the opposite of what every spiritual meme tells you. But here's the thing about spiritual awakening that nobody warns you about - forgiveness isn't the starting line. It's not even the first mile. It's something you arrive at when you're ready, not something you force because you think you're supposed to.

You've probably felt it, right? That heavy pressure to "forgive and move on" before you've even had a chance to feel the weight of what happened. People tell you to let go, to release the resentment, to choose love over anger. And all the while, there's a part of you screaming, "But I'm not done being angry yet. I'm not done grieving. I'm not done being human about this."

That's not a sign you're spiritually broken. That's a sign you're actually doing the real work.

I'm going to be straight with you about what forgiveness actually has to do with spiritual awakening, and it's probably not what you've been told. Does that land? Good. Let's get into it.

Forgiveness Is Not What You Think It Is

Here's the first thing to untangle. Most of us were taught that forgiveness means saying "what you did is okay now." That's not forgiveness. That's surrender dressed up in spiritual clothes.

Real forgiveness doesn't excuse what happened. It doesn't minimize the harm. It doesn't mean you let that person back into your life, or that you pretend the wound doesn't still ache when the weather changes. Real forgiveness is something you do for yourself, not for them. It's the moment you stop letting the past rent space in your present.

I've worked with people who were told they needed to forgive their abusers to "heal." And you know what happened when they tried? They just added shame to the pile. Now they were hurt and failing at forgiveness. Double the weight. Double the pain.

That's not awakening. That's spiritual bypassing with a bow on top.

"Forgiveness isn't about saying what happened was acceptable. It's about deciding that you're done carrying something that was never yours to hold."

When you're in the middle of spiritual awakening, your nervous system is already wide open. You're feeling everything more intensely - the beauty, the grief, the rage, the longing. Adding forced forgiveness on top of that is like trying to put a bandage on a broken bone. It doesn't help. It just hides the injury while it keeps festering underneath.

So let me offer you a different model. One that actually works with the way human beings heal.

The Bright Wound: Why Your Pain Is Part of the Awakening

There's a concept I call the "Bright Wound." It's the idea that the places where you've been broken open are actually the places where the light gets in. Your deepest hurts aren't obstacles to awakening - they're the very doorways through which awakening happens.

Think about it. When did your spiritual journey really begin? Was it when everything was going smoothly and life felt easy? Or was it when something cracked you open - a loss, a betrayal, a realization that the life you were living wasn't actually yours?

For most people, it's the second one. The wound and the awakening are the same thing. You don't get one without the other.

And here's where forgiveness comes in. If you try to forgive too quickly, you close that wound before it's done its work. You seal up the doorway before you've walked through it. You end up with a kind of spiritual scar tissue - a place that looks healed on the outside but is still tender and guarded underneath.

True forgiveness, the kind that actually means something in a spiritual awakening, can only happen after you've let the wound do what it needs to do. After you've felt the full weight of it. After you've let yourself be angry, sad, betrayed, broken, and whatever else comes up.

Does that land? Because I need you to hear this: your anger is not the enemy of your awakening. Your grief is not a failure of your spiritual practice. Your refusal to forgive someone who hasn't earned it is not a sign that you're "stuck." It's a sign that you're honest.

And honesty is the foundation of everything real.

The Three Stages of Real Forgiveness

In my experience, forgiveness in the context of spiritual awakening moves through three stages. You can't skip the first two to get to the third. Trust me, I've seen people try. It doesn't work.

Stage One: Refusal

This is where you're clear: "I will not forgive this. What happened was wrong, and I'm not going to pretend otherwise."

This stage gets a bad reputation in spiritual circles, but it's actually the most honest place you can be. Refusal is a boundary. It's you saying, "My pain is real, and I won't let you spiritualize it away."

Stay here as long as you need to. Feel the refusal fully. Let it have its voice. Write it down. Say it out loud. Let your body know that you're not going to betray yourself by pretending things are fine when they're not.

If you're reading this and thinking, "But I've been in refusal for years," that's okay too. Some wounds take a long time to even begin to process. There's no timeline here. No spiritual graduation date you're supposed to hit.

One thing that helped me during this stage was reading about how other people navigated their own refusal. There's a book called The Gifts of Imperfection by Brené Brown that talks about shame resilience and the courage to be imperfect. It's not a forgiveness book per se, but it helped me understand that I didn't have to have it all figured out. That refusing to fake my way through healing was actually a form of courage. See on Amazon (paid link).

Stage Two: Grief

After refusal has done its work, something shifts. You start to feel the sadness underneath the anger. The grief underneath the refusal.

This is where most people try to run back to anger because grief is harder. Grief doesn't have the fire of righteousness. It's just this quiet, heavy knowing that something was lost that can never be replaced.

But grief is also where the real healing happens. When you let yourself grieve, you're not forgiving anyone. You're not "moving on." You're just letting the wound breathe. You're allowing the pain to exist without trying to fix it or make it go away.

This is the stage where you might find yourself crying at unexpected moments. Where memories surface that you thought you'd buried. Where you feel the full weight of what happened and how it changed you.

It's hard. I'm not going to pretend it isn't. But it's also where the awakening deepens. Because in grief, you're not fighting reality anymore. You're meeting it. And meeting reality without resistance is one of the most spiritual things you can do.

During this stage, I found a lot of comfort in Daring Greatly by Brené Brown. It's about vulnerability and showing up even when you don't know the outcome. Grief is vulnerable. It's showing up without armor. This book helped me see that vulnerability wasn't weakness - it was the only way through. See on Amazon (paid link).

Stage Three: Release

Notice I didn't call this stage "forgiveness." I called it release. Because that's what actually happens when forgiveness is real.

At some point - and you can't force this - you realize that carrying the resentment is hurting you more than it's hurting anyone else. The person who hurt you might not even know you're still carrying it. They might be dead. They might be living their life completely oblivious. And you're the one who's still waking up with that knot in your chest.

Release is when you decide, not because you should, but because you're ready, that you'd rather be free than right.

This isn't about them. It's about you. It's about the exhaustion of holding onto something that's already over. It's about the recognition that your energy is too precious to spend on yesterday's pain.

And here's the beautiful thing - when you release, you don't have to say what happened was okay. You don't have to reconcile. You don't have to let them back in. You just have to stop carrying them around inside you.

That's it. That's the forgiveness that matters in spiritual awakening.

What Forgiveness Looks Like in Practice

So how do you actually do this? How do you move from refusal through grief to release without forcing it?

Here are some things that have worked for people I've worked with, and for me personally:

  • Write a letter you never send. Get it all out. Every ugly, honest, unfiltered thought. Don't censor yourself. Don't try to be spiritual about it. Just write. Then burn it or bury it or keep it in a drawer. The point is that you said it.
  • Let your body have its say. Forgiveness isn't just mental. It's physical. Your body holds the memory of what happened. Sometimes you need to move - shake, cry, punch a pillow, run, dance. Let the body release what the mind can't logic its way out of.
  • Stop timeline-shaming yourself. You don't have to forgive by a certain date. You don't have to be "over it" by next month. Some wounds take years. Some take a lifetime. That's not failure. That's being human.
  • Get support. This is hard work. You don't have to do it alone. A therapist, a trusted friend, a support group - find people who can hold space for you without trying to fix you.

There's a book I came across during my own journey that helped me reframe what healing even means. It's called The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's about how trauma lives in the body and how real healing has to address that physical holding. It's not a light read, but if you're serious about understanding why forgiveness can't just be a mental decision, this book will change how you see everything. See on Amazon (paid link).

When You Forgive and Nothing Changes

I want to be honest about something else. Sometimes you do the work. You move through refusal. You grieve. You release. And you still feel the echo of the pain.

That's normal. That's not a sign you did it wrong.

Forgiveness doesn't erase the memory. It doesn't make you immune to feeling triggered. What it does is change your relationship to the memory. It stops being something that controls you and becomes something that happened to you. There's a difference.

You might still feel a pang when you think about what happened. But it won't have the same charge. It won't send you into a spiral. It'll be more like a scar than an open wound - it's there, you can see it, but it's not bleeding anymore.

And if you're still bleeding? That's okay too. That just means you're not done yet. Go back to stage one. Feel the refusal again. Let the grief come again. There's no rush.

Forgiveness and the Bigger Awakening

Here's what I've come to believe about forgiveness and spiritual awakening. They're not separate things. The ability to forgive - real forgiveness, not the fake kind - is actually a measure of how awake you are.

Because to forgive, you have to see beyond the story. You have to recognize that the person who hurt you was also wounded. That doesn't excuse what they did. But it does put it in a larger context. It lets you see the whole picture instead of just your part of it.

And that's what awakening is. It's seeing more. It's expanding your awareness beyond the narrow story of "me and what happened to me" into something bigger. Something that includes the complexity of being human.

You can hold both things at once: what happened was wrong, AND the person who did it was also struggling. Both can be true. Both are true more often than not.

But you don't have to get there today. You don't have to get there tomorrow. The only requirement is that you stay honest about where you actually are.

That's the real spiritual practice. Not pretending to be more evolved than you are. Not forcing forgiveness before you're ready. Just showing up exactly where you are and letting the process unfold at its own pace.

Your wound is bright. It's not something to hide or rush past. It's the place where your awakening is happening. Trust it. Trust yourself. And when you're ready - not before - forgiveness will be there waiting for you.

If you're curious about where you are in your own journey with forgiveness and awakening, I've put together a short assessment that can help you see the bigger picture. Take the Bright Wound Assessment here to get clarity on your next steps.
